segments.title: Paddle battleship: icebreaking, manoeuvre, and repair
segments.summary: A paddle-wheel icebreaker battleship becomes a design exercise in balancing icebreaking efficiency against manoeuvrability, access for repairs, and ocean-going usefulness.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke weighs whether the bow should shield the paddles, accepting that the arrangement is less efficient in heavy ice. He argues that independently useful manoeuvring paddles, recoverable components, and onboard repair facilities can justify the compromise. The discussion treats a striking fictional hull as a practical question of propulsion, survivability, and maintainability.

evidence.note: The discussion compares paddle protection in ice with manoeuvrability, repair access, and onboard repair facilities.

segments.title: Fictional fleet combat: intelligence, range, and restraint
segments.summary: A science-fiction battle uses uncertain intelligence, turret elevation, and first-shot restraint to show why naval command is more than spotting the enemy first.
segments.body: A commander receives incomplete reports while calculating what differing gun elevations mean for engagement range. The scene brings visual detection, intelligence distribution, and long-range gunnery together with the political cost of firing first. Even with a range advantage, the squadron signals with flares and takes battle stations rather than treating combat as inevitable.

evidence.note: The reading covers advanced technology, sighting range, intelligence on gun elevation, flare signals, and avoiding the first shot.

segments.title: Environmental pressure and neglected naval force multipliers
segments.summary: A discussion of environmental stress becomes a procurement lesson: fleets need tankers, sloops, and other force multipliers as well as the combatants that attract the most attention.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke links the book's treatment of environmental change and large-scale desalination to the way governments postpone expensive but necessary capacity. He compares this to a 1930s choice between destroyers, sloops, and Royal Fleet Auxiliary tankers. Destroyers may seem indispensable, but the smaller escorts and support ships multiply what the fleet can sustain and protect.

evidence.note: He connects environmental pressures and desalination to the interwar trade-off between destroyers, sloops, and fleet auxiliary tankers.

segments.title: Hood's deferred fire-control modernization
segments.summary: HMS Hood's apparently dated fire-control fit in 1941 reflected an incomplete modernization path rather than ignorance of the need for better radar and directors.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that Hood had received only a basic radar fit and was meant to gain better radar and fire-control equipment in a larger refit. The weakness was therefore recognized, but modernization timing left the ship with an interim arrangement in 1941. A later follow-up attributes Hood and Rodney's Dreyer Fire Control Table Mk V installations to when the ships entered service and when their upgrades were carried out. He also notes that fitting Hood's desired four-inch battery would have required finding weight savings elsewhere, showing how modernization plans competed for a finite displacement margin.
segments.question: Why was Hood's fire control considered outdated by 1941?
segments.answerShort: Hood had only a basic radar fit and was awaiting a broader refit with better radar and fire-control equipment; the timing of ship upgrades also shaped which vessels received the Dreyer Mk V table.

evidence.note: He says Hood had only basic radar and that the planned refit included better radar and fire-control equipment.
He links the Dreyer Mk V installations on Hood and Rodney to service-entry and upgrade timing.
He says additional four-inch guns would require fitting fewer weapons or saving weight elsewhere.

segments.title: Why British carrier construction mattered
segments.summary: A shipbuilding counterfactual argues that Britain should have kept carrier construction moving and paused capital ships before sacrificing naval aviation.
segments.body: Asked whether uninterrupted construction would have accelerated British carrier output, Dr. Clarke places carriers ahead of capital ships in the priority order. He ties them to counter-raider operations, anti-submarine warfare, fleet air defence, and Far East deterrence. The answer turns a construction decision into a question about the missions a navy cannot afford to lose.
segments.question: Would uninterrupted British shipbuilding have accelerated carrier output?
segments.answerShort: He would retain carrier construction and pause capital ships first, because carriers supported counter-raider, anti-submarine, air-defence, and Far East deterrence missions.

evidence.note: A viewer asks about carrier output; the answer contrasts capital ships with carrier roles in counter-raider, ASW, air-defence, and Far East deterrence doctrine.

segments.title: Admiralty and Dreyer fire-control tables
segments.summary: The Admiralty Fire Control Table is presented as an improvement in accuracy and the range of data it could incorporate, with an explicit warning that the comparison needs deeper technical treatment.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes the Admiralty Fire Control Table from the Dreyer table mainly through improved accuracy and access to additional data inputs. He is careful not to offer a component-by-component comparison in this brief answer and says the subject needs more detailed treatment. The useful takeaway is therefore limited but clear: the later table refined how a ship combined information for a firing solution. Viewers looking for the precise machinery should treat this moment as a qualified overview rather than a definitive technical history.
segments.question: What was the difference between the Admiralty Fire Control Table and the Dreyer table?
segments.answerShort: Primarily improved accuracy and the ability to factor in more data, although Dr. Clarke flags the answer as a brief overview requiring fuller technical research.

evidence.note: He identifies accuracy and additional data inputs as the main distinction, while saying he plans a more detailed treatment later.

segments.title: The arithmetic behind King George V's 14-inch guns
segments.summary: Britain's 14-inch choice could be defended politically and by a planned twelve-gun broadside, but the case weakened when the finished design carried only ten guns.
segments.body: Asked why Britain chose 14-inch weapons while European rivals announced 15-inch ships, Dr. Clarke first points to the desire to demonstrate political leadership. The naval argument depended on fitting twelve 14-inch guns, whose greater shell volume could plausibly compete with eight 15-inch weapons. Once the design fell to ten 14-inch guns, that volume-of-fire advantage became much less convincing against eight or nine larger guns. The answer shows how a procurement decision can rest on both diplomatic signalling and an armament layout that later changes underneath it.
segments.question: Why did Britain choose 14-inch guns in 1936 when European powers were announcing 15-inch battleships?
segments.answerShort: Political leadership mattered, and a planned twelve-gun 14-inch battery promised a strong volume of fire; the logic became weaker when the ship ended up with ten guns.

evidence.note: He cites political leadership and compares a proposed twelve-gun 14-inch battery with eight or nine 15-inch guns, then notes the weaker ten-gun outcome.

segments.title: Strategy can mean avoiding a catastrophic defeat
segments.summary: Strategic success is not always a decisive victory; sometimes it is preventing a loss whose political and human consequences make any apparent win hollow.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the desire to win a battle with the need to avoid losing one, especially when war appears deceptively binary. He uses nuclear escalation to show how a tactical result can create a worse strategic position: a devastated country, a wider arms race, and enemies with strong reasons for revenge. The distinction gives the surrounding fictional campaign a serious framework for judging risk and restraint.

evidence.note: He explains the difference between winning and avoiding defeat, then discusses why nuclear escalation can make a nominal victory strategically disastrous.

segments.title: Deception can make victory unaffordable
segments.summary: A weaker fleet uses jamming, decoys, uncertain reinforcements, and visible fighter strength to persuade a superior commander that even victory would cost the force needed for the wider war.
segments.body: Walking Stick begins with enough strength to defeat the fifteen ships he can see, even after allowing for serious losses. The opposing commander obscures the picture with jamming and decoys, then introduces a second force whose exact size and identity cannot be confirmed at long range. Confirmed fighter formations prove that some reinforcements are real without revealing whether the apparent twenty capital ships are genuine. Walking Stick could continue and might still win, but his concentrated fleet is the Commonwealth's irreplaceable reserve. His withdrawal demonstrates the moral courage to preserve strategic options when tactical pride and incomplete intelligence tempt a commander into an unaffordable battle.

evidence.note: The passage follows the larger fleet through jamming, decoys, uncertain reinforcements, confirmed fighters, loss calculations, and the decision to withdraw its irreplaceable force.

segments.title: An interwar naval race would reward quality over numbers
segments.summary: Without effective naval treaties, Britain would still answer American and Japanese battleship construction, but the contest would likely emphasize the largest and best ships rather than German-style numerical parity.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the idea that postwar conditions would stop Britain building battleships if other powers continued to build them. He distinguishes the pre-1914 Anglo-German numbers race from a likely 1920s competition among Britain, the United States, and Japan. That later contest would focus on launching the largest and most capable designs rather than matching hull for hull. In practical terms, Britain might complete four G3 battlecruisers and four N3 battleships, making qualitative escalation the central danger that treaty limits were meant to contain.
segments.question: Would postwar conditions have prevented Britain from joining another battleship race if naval treaties failed?
segments.answerShort: No. Britain would build if the United States and Japan did, but the race would probably emphasize ship quality and size rather than the numerical rivalry seen against Germany.

evidence.note: He says Britain would build if others did, contrasts the German numbers race with a British-American-Japanese technology race, and mentions four G3s and four N3s.

segments.title: Missile defence and the destroyer's protective screen
segments.summary: A fictional missile attack turns the destroyer screen into a protection mission built on counter-salvos, jamming, acceleration, and accepting risk to keep a world safe.
segments.body: Five destroyers confront a vast missile swarm with limited magazines, counter-missile salvos, lasers, shields, and the need to close at the right velocity. The reading explicitly recalls the Battle off Samar and treats screening as a warship's duty to place itself between civilians and the enemy. It also shows how jamming, weak targeting data, launcher wear, and shrinking time can defeat an otherwise tidy defensive plan.

evidence.note: The passage covers a destroyer missile screen, counter-salvos, jamming, range and velocity, and an explicit comparison with Samar.

segments.title: Treaty loopholes and a giant pre-dreadnought
segments.summary: A treaty that limits dreadnoughts but leaves pre-dreadnoughts open invites designers to exploit classification rather than accept the agreement's intended restraint.
segments.body: In a speculative treaty scenario, Dr. Clarke imagines a very large ship labelled a pre-dreadnought because it avoids the prohibited all-big-gun form. His answer plays through four 18-inch main guns, 16-inch wing-turret secondaries, and a deliberately mixed armament. The thought experiment shows how legal definitions can distort naval architecture and push designers toward awkward but powerful compromises.
segments.question: How might a treaty that limits dreadnoughts but leaves pre-dreadnoughts open shape a warship design?
segments.answerShort: He imagines a large, nominally pre-dreadnought ship with four 18-inch main guns and 16-inch wing-turret secondaries, exploiting the treaty's classification rather than its intent.

evidence.note: A viewer proposes a treaty loophole; the answer sketches a large non-dreadnought with four 18-inch guns and 16-inch wing-turret secondaries.

segments.title: Fisher's Dreadnought and the value of gun volume
segments.summary: An alternate Dreadnought design shows why gun number and rate of effective fire can matter more than simply fitting the largest possible calibre.
segments.body: Asked whether Fisher might have built HMS Dreadnought with single 16-inch turrets, Dr. Clarke argues that the arrangement would not provide the volume of fire needed at long range. He instead identifies a plausible route in which an earlier British adoption of 14-inch weapons leads to twin 14-inch mounts on Dreadnought. The answer links armament choice to fire-control expectations and the escalation pressure created by a new capital ship.
segments.question: Would Fisher have built HMS Dreadnought with single 16-inch turrets?
segments.answerShort: No. He argues that single 16-inch mounts lack the long-range volume of fire Fisher wanted; an earlier adoption of 14-inch guns could instead have led to twin 14-inch mounts.

evidence.note: A viewer asks about Fisher and single 16-inch turrets; the answer favors gun volume for long-range fire and outlines a 14-inch alternative.

segments.title: Why battleship spotting aircraft remained biplanes
segments.summary: Biplanes remained useful as battleship spotting aircraft because lift and easy takeoff mattered more than the extra speed offered by a monoplane.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes a gunfire-spotting aircraft from a reconnaissance aircraft. A battleship spotter needs to launch reliably, remain near the firing ship, and observe fall of shot; greater speed adds little when the aircraft is effectively flying repeated patterns over the fleet. The answer shows why apparently older aircraft technology could remain well matched to a narrow naval mission.
segments.question: Why was Britain slow to adopt monoplanes as battleship spotting aircraft?
segments.answerShort: Because biplanes offered strong lift and easy takeoff, while a monoplane's extra speed brought little benefit to an aircraft flying local spotting patterns rather than long-range reconnaissance.

evidence.note: The answer compares biplane lift and takeoff performance with the limited value of monoplane speed for battleship spotting rather than reconnaissance.

segments.title: How escorts reacted to the loss of HMS Hood
segments.summary: Ships did develop a sense of grievance after the loss of close consorts, with Hood's escorts and Tribal-class destroyers offered as an example.
segments.body: Asked whether ships ever sought revenge for a lost sister or intended sister, Dr. Clarke broadens the idea beyond formal class relationships. He points to escorts associated with HMS Hood, including Tribal-class destroyers, as ships whose crews were deeply angered by her destruction. The exchange is a compact reminder that operational relationships and shared service could create bonds as meaningful as design lineage.
segments.question: Did ships or intended sister ships ever seek revenge for a vessel that had been sunk?
segments.answerShort: Yes. He notes that Hood's escorts and Tribal-class destroyers were not literal sisters, but their crews were intensely angered by her loss.

evidence.note: He answers affirmatively and cites Hood's escorts and Tribal-class destroyers as vessels angered by her sinking.

segments.title: Dual-purpose guns and the limits of improving ABDACOM
segments.summary: Better dual-purpose guns would improve Allied air defence in the early Pacific war, but the effect still depends on the composition of the whole ABDACOM force.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke agrees that fitting British and Australian ships with twin 4.7-inch dual-purpose weapons would make them more capable against air attack. He cautions that equipment cannot be assessed in isolation, because the result changes dramatically with the ships and force structure actually available to ABDACOM. The answer separates a clear tactical improvement from claims that it would transform the campaign by itself.
segments.question: How would twin 4.7-inch dual-purpose guns on British and Australian ships affect the Pacific war and ABDACOM?
segments.answerShort: They would improve air-defence capability, but the wider effect depends heavily on what overall force ABDACOM has available.

evidence.note: He says the ships would be more capable against air attack but stresses that ABDACOM's complete force composition determines the larger effect.

segments.title: Why mechanical fire-control computers endured
segments.summary: The Difference Engine's mechanical fire-control premise is plausible because real mechanical fire-control tables remained effective for decades.
segments.body: Discussing a fictional alternate computer, Dr. Clarke notes that mechanical fire-control tables remained useful into the 1960s. For large naval guns, they lasted even longer because replacing a mature system required designing and funding a better solution from scratch. The exchange grounds a science-fiction device in the real durability of purpose-built analogue computation.
segments.question: What would limit a Babbage-style mechanical computer used for naval fire control?
segments.answerShort: Not as much as one might expect: mechanical fire-control tables remained effective into the 1960s and persisted especially long for large guns because few navies funded a wholly new replacement.

evidence.note: He compares the fictional machine with real mechanical fire-control tables used into the 1960s and later for large guns.

segments.title: Advanced fire control could bring the dreadnought idea forward
segments.summary: Giving 1890s fleets effective long-range fire control could encourage dreadnought-style armament before turbine propulsion was ready.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that improved computation would let pre-dreadnought-era ships make practical use of their long-range guns. That capability could produce a vessel with a dreadnought-like gun arrangement during the 1890s, even if its machinery remained conventional. The answer shows how fire control, rather than propulsion alone, could shift the logic of capital-ship design.
segments.question: How would advanced mechanical fire control change fleets of the 1890s and early 1900s?
segments.answerShort: It would make long-range gunnery usable and could bring about a dreadnought-style gun layout in the 1890s, even without dreadnought-style machinery.

evidence.note: He says better fire control would make long-range guns usable and could produce a dreadnought-style gun layout in the 1890s.

segments.title: Port Stanley if von Spee arrived before Sturdee
segments.summary: If von Spee reached Port Stanley first, shore-obscured British battleship fire would probably drive him away and turn the battle into an ocean search.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke imagines the German squadron approaching Port Stanley before Admiral Sturdee's force arrives. A British battleship in harbour could fire using spotting arrangements while von Spee's ships lacked the means to see and answer a target hidden beyond the hill. Spee would likely withdraw, leaving Sturdee to refuel and begin a much less certain ocean pursuit.
segments.question: What happens if von Spee reaches Port Stanley before Sturdee?
segments.answerShort: A battleship in harbour could engage over the hill while von Spee could not effectively reply, probably forcing him back to sea and turning Sturdee's operation into an ocean search.

evidence.note: He describes a British battleship firing from Port Stanley, von Spee's inability to spot over the hill, and a subsequent ocean pursuit by Sturdee.

segments.title: A 1940 war gives Britain an escort advantage
segments.summary: Delaying the outbreak of war by a year would enlarge Britain's anti-submarine escort force faster than Germany could enlarge its U-boat arm in peacetime.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke estimates that a September 1940 outbreak could put roughly sixty additional British escorts into service. German submarine production under another year of peacetime conditions would not rise at the same rate, while British anti-submarine construction was already positioned to expand sharply. The counterfactual highlights how mobilization timing can change the balance between raiders and the numerous smaller ships needed to contain them.
segments.question: How would a September 1940 start to the Second World War affect the naval balance?
segments.answerShort: Britain could have roughly sixty more escorts in service, while German peacetime submarine production would grow much less, creating a major anti-submarine advantage for the Royal Navy.

evidence.note: He estimates about sixty extra Royal Navy escorts by September 1940 and contrasts their production growth with German peacetime submarine output.

segments.title: Service identity as a military institution
segments.summary: A fictional admiral's worldview illustrates how service and regimental identity can divide insiders from the civilians they protect.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recognizes in the novel a strong institutional loyalty found across real armed forces. Membership in the same navy, army, branch, or regiment can mark someone as especially important, while everyone outside the group becomes a person to be protected rather than an equal insider. The observation helps readers notice how military cohesion can be both a source of duty and a narrowing lens through which characters understand society.

evidence.note: He compares the novel's naval identity with real service and regimental cultures that privilege insiders while defining civilians as people to protect.

segments.title: Strategic intelligence means deciding with an incomplete picture
segments.summary: A science-fiction battle becomes a lesson in combining incomplete intelligence about the battlefield, the enemy commander, outside actors, and one's own irreplaceable force.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that the scene works because neither commander possesses a complete picture. Strategic intelligence must combine events across an entire system with knowledge of the opponent, the intentions of third parties, and the limitations of one's own fleet. The decisive constraint is that Walking Stick has concentrated everything he has: even a likely tactical victory can be unacceptable when no reserve remains to absorb losses or answer the next threat.

evidence.note: He identifies incomplete strategic and battlefield intelligence, knowledge of opponents, other factions, and the lack of any reserve fleet as the scene's teaching value.

segments.title: Why Britain occupied Iceland in 1940
segments.summary: Britain occupied Iceland to prevent a small German seizure from creating an air and naval base that would endanger Atlantic convoys and require a costly amphibious recovery.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Germany did not need a massive invasion to create a strategic crisis; a reinforced battalion landed from a merchant ship might have secured an island with no armed forces of its own. German-held Iceland could serve as an unsinkable aircraft carrier in the North Atlantic, exposing convoys and forcing Britain into a difficult amphibious campaign. The British occupation therefore traded an acknowledged violation of Icelandic neutrality for a lower-risk way to deny Germany the base, after which American forces could assume much of the garrison burden.
segments.question: Why did Britain occupy Iceland when Germany was unlikely to mount a large amphibious invasion?
segments.answerShort: Because even a small German force might seize the undefended island and turn it into an Atlantic air base, forcing Britain to retake it at far greater cost to convoys, troops, and Icelandic civilians.

evidence.note: He describes a possible small German seizure, Iceland as an Atlantic aircraft base, convoy danger, the cost of retaking it, and the later American garrison.

segments.title: Fire-control backups from duplicate tables to local control
segments.summary: Warships backed up centralized fire control with duplicate positions, local gun control, and highly trained officers capable of estimating corrections themselves.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that larger ships could carry more than one fire-control table or position, while smaller vessels relied on the fact that serious damage often drove combat into shorter ranges. At close range, guns could shift to local control rather than depending on the long-range calculation system. He cites HMS Exeter's gunnery officer directing a surviving gun from atop its turret and estimating corrections by observation, showing that trained people formed part of the redundancy.
segments.question: What backup existed if a warship's main fire-control system failed?
segments.answerShort: Ships could use duplicate fire-control positions, shift to local control at shorter range, and rely on trained gunnery officers to estimate corrections without the main table.

evidence.note: He discusses duplicate tables, local control at close range, and HMS Exeter's gunnery officer directing a gun from the turret.

segments.title: How a German attack on Ireland could aid Atlantic convoys
segments.summary: A German airborne attack on Ireland would be hard to sustain and could push Ireland into the Allied camp, opening valuable Atlantic bases for convoy and anti-submarine operations.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke stresses the distance and exposure involved in flying German troops around or across Britain, followed by the harder problem of reinforcing them. The attack would also confront Irish forces and veterans rather than an empty objective. Strategically, making Ireland an enemy could give Britain access to Irish airfields, convoy assembly points, and west-coast bases for hunter-killer groups, improving Atlantic coverage rather than weakening it.
segments.question: What would happen if Germany attempted an airborne attack on Ireland?
segments.answerShort: The force would be difficult to deliver and reinforce, while the attack could bring Ireland into the war and open its airfields and Atlantic ports to Allied convoy and anti-submarine forces.

evidence.note: He covers the flight and reinforcement problem, Irish resistance, and the possibility of Allied convoy and air bases across Ireland.
He adds that Irish west-coast harbours could stage hunter-killer groups supporting Atlantic convoys.

segments.title: Why science fiction calls so many warships cruisers
segments.summary: Science fiction borrows the word cruiser because historically it described a flexible cruising role as well as a broad place below capital ships in the fleet hierarchy.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that real cruisers performed an unusually wide range of independent and fleet duties. The name therefore gives a fictional designer room to fill almost any medium or large non-capital surface-combatant role without defining a precise historical analogue. Terms such as artillery cruiser work because the underlying label has always mixed mission, status, and size.
segments.question: Why do science-fiction games use so many different types of vessel called cruisers?
segments.answerShort: Because cruiser historically described a broad cruising role as much as a fixed size, making it a flexible label for the largest non-capital surface combatants in a fictional fleet.

evidence.note: He explains cruiser as both a cruising role and a broad size or status category below capital ships, making it useful fictional terminology.

segments.title: How a 14-inch Dreadnought could accelerate gun escalation
segments.summary: Beginning HMS Dreadnought with 14-inch guns could force later British classes toward 16-inch weapons, triple turrets, or still larger calibres much earlier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows the knock-on effects of starting the dreadnought era one calibre higher. The next generation would need a visible improvement, making 15- or 16-inch guns plausible for the Orions and leaving the Queen Elizabeth class to seek another jump. The alternatives—larger guns or triple 16-inch turrets—show how one early design decision could compress the historical escalation in battleship armament.
segments.question: What follows if HMS Dreadnought begins with twin 14-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: Later classes would need to escalate sooner, potentially moving the Orions toward 16-inch guns and forcing the Queen Elizabeth class toward triple 16-inch mounts or 17- to 18-inch weapons.

evidence.note: He traces the calibre progression from a 14-inch Dreadnought to possible 16-inch Orions and larger or triple-gun Queen Elizabeth designs.

segments.title: Why large space warships become multirole ships
segments.summary: A large space warship may plausibly combine battleship, carrier, and assault-ship functions because a distant task force needs its biggest hulls to do many jobs.
segments.body: Asked whether Battlestars lack a clear identity, Dr. Clarke argues that their mixed role may be exactly what space operations demand. Once a navy builds a very large ship to deploy far from support, it will want that hull to fight, carry aircraft, transport forces, and answer varied emergencies. The fictional design therefore resembles a task group's concentrated capability more than a single historical ship type.
segments.question: Are Battlestars incoherent because they combine battleship, carrier, and assault-ship roles?
segments.answerShort: Not necessarily. Large space warships may need to do everything a distant force requires, making a multirole capital ship a plausible outcome.

evidence.note: He says large ships in space would probably need to perform many roles because they must arrive able to do whatever the operation requires.

segments.title: Navigation lights in a blacked-out convoy
segments.summary: Wartime ships could run completely dark, but convoys often retained extremely dim navigation lights to reduce the danger of colliding with one another.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes full blackout from the practical needs of ships steaming in company. Individual vessels might show no navigation lights at all, but convoy ships were expected to use carefully screened, very dim lights at appropriate times. The compromise balanced concealment from the enemy against the immediate navigational risk created by tightly grouped merchantmen.
segments.question: What was the wartime protocol for navigation lights on merchant ships?
segments.answerShort: Ships could be completely blacked out, but convoys generally tried to retain very dim navigation lights so vessels could avoid colliding with one another.

evidence.note: He contrasts complete blackout with the very dim navigation lights used in convoys to prevent collisions.
